label: fiction
title: The Locked Gallery
charge: Theft of a jewel collection from a gallery strongroom

beats.text: The investigating detective, called by the defence: the accused discovered the theft, raised the alarm, and has cooperated with every request since. 'In my experience, thieves don't summon the police.'
beats.reveal_note: In everyone's experience — which is the move's whole value. Controlling the discovery controls the clock, the scene, and the first story everyone hears.

beats.text: A systems auditor on the vault's records: an integrity gap — entries missing, checksums broken — spanning ninety minutes, exactly inside the accused's badge-logged 'system test' the night before the theft was reported.
beats.reveal_note: Logs don't develop gaps by coincidence inside a maintenance window keyed to one man's badge. The system recorded who silenced it.

beats.text: A master locksmith for the defence: the strongroom cannot be breached from outside. 'Not by me — not by anyone I have ever met.'
beats.reveal_note: Precisely. Evidence that no outsider could get in is evidence about insiders — the defence proved the prosecution's geometry.

beats.text: An invoice trail: museum-grade paste replicas of eleven pieces from the collection, commissioned months earlier, paid from the accused's card, delivered to a storage unit rented in his name.
beats.reveal_note: Nobody rehearses a theft they haven't planned. The replicas are intent — itemised and receipted.

beats.text: The judge: exclusive opportunity is powerful evidence where access truly was exclusive. The jury should first decide who could — then ask who did.
beats.reveal_note: The funnel of the case: the locksmith shrank 'who could' to a handful; the log gap and the replicas picked the man.

twist: The impossible crime was the confession: a vault only an insider could open, a log silenced during his own badge-logged test, and paste replicas of the collection invoiced to his card months in advance. He raised the alarm because the first voice at the scene writes the story — it's the oldest move there is.
